name: Headshots at Home: A DIY Lighting and Backdrop Guide

step.name: Find your window and clear the room
step.text: Pick the biggest window in your home with indirect daylight. A north-facing window is ideal. Turn off every other light in the room to avoid mixed colour temperatures.

step.name: Set up a clean background
step.text: Find a plain wall opposite the window. White, light grey or warm beige works well. Clear away any clutter. Iron a bedsheet if using one as a backdrop.

step.name: Position yourself relative to the window
step.text: Stand three to four feet from the window with the window to one side. Turn your shoulders at about a 30-degree angle to the camera. Turn your face a little further toward the light.

step.name: Add a reflector on the shadow side
step.text: Place something white opposite the window to bounce light into the shadow side of your face. Keep it within two feet of your face for best effect.

step.name: Set up your camera at eye level
step.text: Your camera must be at your eye level. Use a tripod or stack of books. Use the rear camera on a phone, not the selfie camera, with portrait mode and the timer.

step.name: Shoot in bursts of three to five
step.text: Set the timer to 10 seconds. Take a test shot, check framing and exposure, then shoot 20 to 30 frames varying your expression subtly.

step.name: Review, edit lightly, and pick three
step.text: Review on a proper screen. Shortlist five or six. Edit lightly — brightness, contrast, gentle sharpening around the eyes. Avoid heavy filters. Pick your best three.
